data.title: Microsoft, Meta Set To Report Earnings As Investors Watch Out For Trump-Xi, Fed Meeting
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street opened on a positive note Friday, with the S&P 500 hitting a record intraday high after September's consumer price index came in slightly cooler than expected. The long-delayed report, postponed by the ongoing U.S. government shutdown, showed CPI rising 0.3% month-over-month-below both the 0.4% consensus and August's 0.4% increase. Investor sentiment was further lifted by strong results from Intel, whose shares jumped 5.7% in early trading after the chipmaker posted third-quarter earnings and guidance that beat expectations. The upbeat open followed Thursday's gains, when markets rallied as investors digested Tesla's results and news that President Donald Trump will meet with President Xi next Thursday.

Amid a U.S. government shutdown, investors will keep a close eye on economic reports that will be released next week; however, government numbers are in doubt as the shutdown continues. The economic reports that are expected to be announced next week include reports on Core Durable Goods Orders, Dallas Fed Mfg Business Index, and Atlanta Fed GDP, with reports on Redbook and Richmond Manufacturing Index releasing on Tuesday. Wednesday will see the release of MBA Mortgage Applications, Pending Home Sales, and the FOMC Interest Rate Statement, with Core PCE Prices releasing on Thursday. Employment Benefits and Personal Spending are the main reports scheduled to be announced on Friday.

Apple (AAPL), Amazon.com (AMZN), Eli Lilly (LLY), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Meta Platforms (META), Caterpillar (CAT), ServiceNow (NOW), Boeing (BA), Exxon Mobil (XOM), AbbVie (ABBV), Chevron (CVX), Charter Communications (CHTR), Visa (V), and UnitedHealth (UNH) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, Oct 27: Waste Management (WM), Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP), Brown & Brown (BRO), FTAI Aviation (FTAI), F5 Networks (FFIV). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, Oct 28: Visa, UnitedHealth, NextEra Energy (NEE), Booking (BKNG), United Parcel Service (UPS), PayPal (PYPL), MSCI (MSCI). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, Oct 29: Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta Platforms, Caterpillar, ServiceNow, Boeing. See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, Oct 30: Apple, Amazon.com, Eli Lilly, Mastercard (MA), Reddit (RDDT). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, Oct 31: Exxon Mobil, AbbVie, Chevron, Charter Communications. See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Inhibrx Biosciences (INBX) and Deckers Outdoor (DECK) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Investview (OTCQB:INVU), Armata Pharmaceuticals (ARMP), and Tourmaline Bio (TRML). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Integer (ITGR), Ready Capital (RC), and United Homes Group (UHG). Beyond Meat (BYND) and Kohl's (KSS) head into the week with a high level of short interest outstanding and social media buzz active.

IPO watch: Companies expected to price their IPOs and begin trading next week include clinical-stage biopharmaceutical firm MapLight Therapeutics (MPLT), HW Electro KK (HWEP), and travel tech company Navan (NAVN).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Realty Income (O), Morgan Stanley (MS), Cal-Maine (CALM), and Citizens Financial (CFG).

Microsoft Earnings Preview: Microsoft is set to announce its first-quarter earnings next week, with analysts expecting the company to report earnings per share of $3.66 on revenue of $75.37B. Analysts expect another clean beat and little room for disappointment. All eyes are on Azure's reacceleration as AI workloads turn into real demand despite ongoing capacity limits. Analysts expect CapEx to remain elevated but strategically deployed, keeping the long-term AI narrative credible rather than inflated.

Meta Earnings Preview: Analysts expect Meta to report earnings per share of $6.64 on revenue of $49.36B, with a primary focus on the AI market and the company's AI monetization. Meta's broader trend remains underpinned by investor confidence in its AI expansion and data center build-out. Meanwhile, the partnership with Arm comes at a crucial time for Meta, which has faced a volatile trading month amid market-wide profit-taking in large-cap tech. This pivot is increasingly significant as the competition in generative and recommendation-based AI intensifies, with peers like Google and Amazon also ramping up infrastructure spending.

data.title: Retire On Income: Revealing 2 Of My Holdings Up To 9% Cash Per Year
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

"What is Jerusalem worth?" - Balian of Ibelin

"Nothing." - Saladin

Takes a few steps, raises his arms, smiles, and says

"Everything."

That famous scene from Kingdom of Heaven captures a fundamental element in human psychology: Value is never fixed; it is defined by circumstance.

Can something really be worth both nothing and everything at the same time? It is always circumstantial. Food at the airport costs triple what it would anywhere else. Similarly, popcorn at a theater or theme park sells for 10-20 times its actual value. You would spend $2.99/Gal to fill your tank at your neighborhood gas station, but if you are stranded with an empty tank in the middle of nowhere (and you didn't invest in a good roadside assistance plan), prepare to pay whatever they ask. It would only cost a few bucks to make a spare key at Home Depot. However, losing your only key at 11 p.m. will require you to pay a locksmith a small fortune.

Value is never absolute. It's relative to timing, need, and circumstance.

Retirement is no different.

When you retire, you stop receiving regular paychecks from your employer, but you still need income to tackle bills, maintain your lifestyle, and handle surprise expenses. If your retirement plan depends on you manually selling shares at regular intervals, then market volatility becomes a source of anxiety.

That's why at High Dividend Opportunities, we focus on steady, reliable income from essential market sectors. Prices go up and down, but I can count on my portfolio to produce the cash flow I need all the time, not just when the market sentiment is bullish. With that in mind, let's look at our top picks.

Pick #1: CIM Baby Bonds - Up To 9% Yields

Chimera Investment Corp. (CIM) is an mREIT focused primarily on a diversified portfolio of mortgage assets, particularly residential mortgage loans, agency and non-agency MBS (Mortgage Backed Securities), and MSR (Mortgage Servicing Rights). As of June 30, 2025, CIM's assets were allocated 74% to residential loans (including 66% in reperforming loans, which are loans that were previously delinquent but brought back to current standing), 19% to Agency MBS, and 7% to Non-Agency RMBS. Source

The mREIT places significant emphasis on re-performing loans. As such, the average FICO score of its residential loan portfolio stood at 669 at the end of Q2. However, CIM maintains exposure to over 99,600 loans, with a weighted-average maturity of 20.5 years, and average loan-to-value at origination of 77.6% (and 63% amortized). About 83% of CIM's loans are issued against properties that are owner-occupied.

The mREIT ended the second quarter with $250 million in cash and cash equivalents, and the company announced the acquisition of a leading non-qualified mortgage lender, HomeXpress, to be in a position to create and originate their own mortgages, while controlling the quality and loan terms. This bolt-on acquisition, which was completed on October 1st, will also allow the mREIT to generate more securitizations and invest in them, instead of only buying them.

In recent years, CIM has been deleveraging well, bringing its total leverage down to 4.5x at the end of the second quarter. During the Q2 conference call, management noted their intent to diversify their income streams, and grow assets, to support and grow their dividend over time.

"We'll continue diversifying our portfolio and income streams, growing recurring fee income, adding liquidity and looking for opportunities to add accretive platforms and invest in accretive assets, all with the focus of growing our assets and dividend or total economic return over the long term." - Phillip John Kardis, President & CEO

We are interested in CIM's three public baby bonds, which offer high yields while presenting excellent safety for investors.

  • 9.000% Senior Notes due 5/15/2029 (CIMN) - Yield 8.9%

  • 9.250% Senior Notes due 8/15/2029 (CIMO) - Yield 9%

  • 8.875% Senior Notes due 8/15/2030 (CIMP) - Yield 8.8%

CIM's baby bonds carry BBB ratings from Egan-Jones, with maturities in 2029 and 2030. CIMN and CIMO become callable next year, in May and August, respectively; while CIMP isn't callable until August 2027, providing a runway for steady income. CIMP presents the best opportunity for investors at this time.

CIM spent $42.7 million on preferred dividends during 1H 2024, covered by $202 million in net income (which is calculated after factoring in interest expenses). The mREIT's liquidity position is strong enough to support business growth and shareholder/debtholder commitments.

Pick #2: SCHW Preferreds - Up To 6% Yields

Charles Schwab (SCHW) is the largest U.S. retail broker, with 45 million client accounts and $11.6 trillion in client assets as of October 2025. The company offers its individual and institutional clients a wide range of financial services, like banking, brokerage, wealth management, and advisory services.

SCHW recently reported a solid third quarter, with a 14% YoY increase in total AUM (Assets Under Management), a whopping 39% YoY increase ($80.3 billion) in core net new assets. SCHW company benefits from increased market volatility through higher trading revenues. During Q3, the company facilitated 7.4 million daily average trades and reported a 25% YoY increase in trading revenues. On the other side of this, SCHW enjoys steady fee-based revenues in its wealth and asset management segments, with 13% YoY growth in administration and management fees to $1.7 billion.

During the third quarter, the company spent $2.7 billion to repurchase 28.9 million shares, and this brings the YTD total to $8.5 billion towards share repurchases. With this, the company still maintains adequate capacity from its authorized $20 billion share repurchase plan.

SCHW has been a terrific dividend steward since its inception, with periodic payout increases. However, its current annual yield stands at a paltry 1.1%, making it less desirable for our passive income needs.

We will explore higher in the company's financial structure, for greater yields at discounted price points. SCHW has two public perpetual preferreds, rated "BBB" by S&P, and offering yields of up to 5.9%.

  • 5.95% Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock Series D (SCHW.PR.D) - Yield 5.9%

  • 4.45% Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock Series J (SCHW.PR.J) - Yield 5.6%

About $343 million was spent on preferred stock dividends during the first nine months of 2025, while generating $6 billion in net income during this period (SCHW reported this net income after factoring in preferred dividend expenses). At this time, SCHW-J offers a CD-beating 5.6% QDI yield, and ~25% upside to par.

SCHW prefers to enjoy excellent coverage from the company's predictably growing operations, and is well-positioned for upside with interest-rate cuts.

Conclusion

Valuation is an art, not a science. And value is always determined by circumstance. Whether it is a snack, a tank of gas, or a spare key, none of these command premium pricing until desperation or lack of options strikes. Your retirement income works the same way.

During good times, companies provide healthy forecasts, and the markets are jumping in excitement. Investors consider themselves geniuses and convince themselves that selling shares will always be a reasonable, available, and sustainable option. However, it is important to remember that most investors today haven't seen a serious recession for decades. It is easy to say things have evolved and that the government will come to the rescue of the markets, or that this time is different. You say whatever you want to convince yourself that stocks only go up. When volatility strikes, or when life throws an unexpected bill your way, your ability to rely on your portfolio for reliable cash flow becomes the difference between confidence and panic.

At High Dividend Opportunities, our focus isn't on predicting the next rally or timing exits. It's on securing income from sectors that provide essential functions and have proven their resilience across economic cycles. Reliable income turns uncertainty into choice, and it is what every retiree truly values.

data.title: Intesa: Not Much Value Beyond Its 6.2% Dividend Yield
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, Intesa Sanpaolo (OTCPK:ISNPY)(OTCPK:IITSF) is a quality bank in the European banking sector, plus it historically offered a high-dividend yield that was quite attractive to income-oriented investors. Since my last article on the bank, some couple of years ago, its share price performance has been quite impressive, being up by more than 190% including dividends, beating the overall stock market during the same period by a wide margin.

As I’ve not covered Intesa for some time and the bank is scheduled to report its quarterly earnings related to Q3 2025 in the coming days, I think it’s now a good time to revisit its investment case and update its financial performance, to see if it still offers value for investors following a strong share price rally in recent years.

Earnings Analysis

Intesa has reported a positive operating performance over the past few quarters, despite headwinds from lower market rates due to the European Central Bank (ECB) declining rate path from the middle of 2024 to last June. As shown in the next graph, the ECB cut several times its key rate from a peak level of 4.5% in 2024, to its current level of 2.15% that has been unchanged over the past few months.

While Intesa is not among the European banks more exposed to rates, as the bank’s diversification strategy has resulted in a diversified revenue mix compared to some other banks, it’s not immune to lower market rates. Moreover, contrary to other European countries where loans are largely set at fixed-rates, variable-rate loans dominate in Italy. This makes banks in the country more exposed to changes in interest rates than banks operating in Central European countries or Scandinavia, for instance.

This explains why Intesa’s net interest income (NII) declined by 6.8% YoY during the first half of 2025 (H1 2025) to €7.4 billion, showing that rates are now a headwind for the bank’s top-line performance. However, fees, commissions, and insurance income performed well, delivering higher revenues in H1 2025 compared to the same period last year. Its overall revenues amounted to €13.8 billion in the first semester (+1.1% YoY), showing that strength in other revenues was enough to offset NII weakness. NII’s weight on total revenues was about 54% in the period, showing that Intesa is not very reliant on NII compared to other retail-oriented banks (which have weights above 75%), but its variable-rate loan book makes it exposed to declining market rates.

During Q3 2025, the Euribor rate (which usually is used as the benchmark for variable-rate loans) traded sideways between 1.95%-2.05%, which means the bank’s NII is likely to be more stable on a quarterly basis than in the previous quarters. Indeed, according to analysts’ estimates, its NII should be around €3.7 billion in Q3 2025, down by just 2% quarter-on-quarter, while on an annual basis this represents a decline of 3.8%. For the following quarters, the street expects Intesa’s NII to be between €3.7-3.8 billion in Q4 and through 2026, as the market is assuming a stable ECB rate until the end of next year.

Regarding other revenue lines, the street is forecasting some growth on fees and commissions over the next few quarters, but lower income from insurance operations, showing that Intesa’s revenue growth prospects are somewhat muted. Its quarterly revenues are expected to be around €7 billion over the next four quarters, not being very different from what the bank has reported in Q2 2025.

Regarding costs, the bank has reported good cost control during a period of inflationary pressures, which has pushed staff costs higher in recent quarters. Despite that, Intesa’s operating expenses declined by 0.2% YoY to €5.24 billion in H1 2025, leading to positive operating jaws (revenue growth minus cost growth) in the period. This was positive for a slight improvement in the bank’s operating efficiency, measured by the cost-to-income (C/I) ratio that declined by 50 basis points (bps) to 38% in Q2. This is a very good level of efficiency compared to other European banks, being justified by Intesa’s investments in digitalization and rationalization of its branch network in recent years, but also by seasonal factors (high-interest rates in recent years).

Due to this combination of slightly higher revenues and good cost control, its pre-provision profit increased by 1.9% YoY to more than €8.5 billion in the first half of 2025, which can be considered a good result considering that rates are now a headwind for earnings growth. However, over the next few quarters, its pre-provision profit is expected to be relatively flat compared to its recent history, reflecting the bank’s muted growth prospects and top-line headwind from lower rates.

Regarding its credit quality, it has remained quite good as the Italian economy has been resilient, being positive for the credit performance of consumers and SMEs. Reflecting good credit quality despite some macroeconomic uncertainties, including, for instance, the impact of the U.S. trade war on Italian exports, Intesa’s provisions for loan losses declined by 8.8% YoY, to €505 million in H1 2025.

Given this backdrop of slightly higher operating income and lower provisions for loan losses, plus lower extraordinary costs (lower by €236 million in the first semester compared to H1 2024) related to integration costs and exit incentives for staff, Intesa’s net income increased by 9% YoY to more than €5.2 billion in H1 2025. This is a new record for the first half of the year, showing that Intesa delivered a good performance during a tough period, with some positive effects offsetting the negative impact of lower rates in the bank’s NII.

Given this resilient performance, Intesa raised its guidance for the full year, expecting now to deliver a net income well above €9 billion. This represents annual growth of at least 4% YoY, while the street is expecting a net income of about €9.3 billion in 2025 (+7% YoY). This means its net income is expected to decline in the second half of the year compared to H1 2025, which is explained by the street estimating higher provisions for loan losses in Q3 and Q4. Indeed, for the second half of the year, provisions are expected to be around €850 million, up by 68% from the previous semester, justified by weaker economic conditions expected ahead and some impact of U.S. tariffs on Italian corporations. Additionally, the bank usually books in Q4 the impact of restructuring measures, namely friendly staff exits, that are expected to result in a lower net income in the second half of the year. Despite that, Intesa’s profitability is expected to remain at very high levels given that its return on equity (ROE) ratio was 20% in H1 2025 and is expected to be around 15% for the full year (vs. 17% in 2024), being a very good profitability level within the European banking sector.

On top of a good profitability, Intesa also has a strong capital position, with both factors being important for the bank’s attractive capital returns policy. Its CET1 ratio was 13.5% at the end of last June, a level that the bank sees as comfortable over the medium term, allowing it to distribute most annual earnings to shareholders. Its dividend policy is to distribute 70% of annual earnings through dividends and share buybacks, to maintain its capital ratio between 13-14% and return ‘excess capital’ to shareholders.

Its last annual dividend (related to 2024 earnings) was €0.341 per share, paid in two installments, leading to a dividend yield of around 6.2% at its current share price. This is much lower compared to when I last analyzed Intesa (it was yielding 11% at the time), showing that its shares no longer appear to be undervalued. While Intesa’s dividend yield is still attractive to income-oriented investors and is slightly above the average of the European banking sector, Intesa offered in the past a much higher dividend yield than peers, something that is no longer true.

Showing that Intesa is no longer undervalued following an impressive share price rally over the past couple of years, its valuation is nowadays much higher than it was in the past. As shown in the next graph, Intesa is trading at the top of its historical range over the past decade at around 1.4x book value (vs. 0.8x book value when I last analyzed it), a multiple that seems stretched as the bank’s earnings should be at a peak and momentum in the near future is not expected to be impressive.

Compared to peers, Intesa trades at a very similar valuation to its closest competitor, UniCredit (OTCPK:UNCRY), but it’s trading at a significant premium to Banco BPM (OTCPK:BNCZF) that is trading at only 1.17x book value. Compared to other European banks with a retail-oriented business model, such as ABN AMRO (OTCPK:AAVMY), Lloyds Banking Group (LYB), or ING Groep (ING), Intesa is also trading at a premium valuation, showing that its shares appear to be somewhat overvalued right now.

Conclusion

Intesa has reported a resilient operating performance during the first half of 2025, but operating momentum is not expected to be very bullish ahead, as rates are now a headwind for top-line growth and provisions for loan losses are likely to go up from low levels in recent quarters. Despite that, Intesa is trading at a high valuation compared to its own history and compared to peers, making its high dividend yield the best reason to hold its shares for the time being.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PennantPark Floating Rate Capital: I'm Buying This ~13% Yield Now
data.description:
PennantPark Floating Rate Capital (NYSE:PFLT) is one of the many BDCs that have been thrown into the discount rack by the market.

On a YTD basis, PFLT has lost about 18% of its market cap, which has resulted in a P/NAV of 0.77x. This is more or less in line with the sector-wide pricing considering that the sector median P/NAV is 0.78x. Because of the significant price drop, PFLT's dividend yield has jumped to 13.7%.

It is clear that the yield here is unsustainable and sooner or later will get cut. The same applies to the discount angle - the bulk of the discount is driven by systematic pressures, which, in my opinion, are justified. The base rates are already 25 basis points lower than going into 2025 and the consensus expectations indicate a series of near-term cuts. At the same time, the spreads continue to tighten amid subdued LBO activity and mushrooming supply of fresh capital earmarked to new private credit opportunities. On top of that the risks in the economy are mounting. It would be quite odd to argue against having some form of discounts in place.

Yet, PFLT seems to be one of the few BDC players that have been punished too hard. I think that the market has overlooked several nuances that separate PFLT from most of the peers. These nuances, which I will describe below, collectively make a strong case for relative outperformance.

Again, my expectation is not that PFLT's share price suddenly converges back to NAV or that the dividend at this level remains unchanged over, say, the next 2-3 quarters. Instead, the case boils down to profiting from stronger price recovery when the overall sector begins to move in the other (positive) direction, and capturing relatively de-risked current income streams, which even after the dividend cut would remain fairly attractive.

Thesis review

I will start with a rather broad element, which is something that sits right at the core of PFLT's value proposition and my bullish thesis.

The thing is that PFLT operates in the core middle market, where the average EBITDA generation for an investment company is between $10 million to $50 million. Many of the larger and well-known peers are operating in the upper middle market, where EBITDA generation for a typical company is significantly above $50 million. Plus, this is exactly the place, where the lion's share of new private credit capital formation takes place as the institutional giants simply have to operate with meaningful ticket size deals in order to avoid bloat and generate scale efficiencies.

Now, what this means is that the upper middle market segment has naturally become subject to unfavorable supply/demand dynamics. Namely, the bigger the supply, the stronger the pressure on spreads (i.e., profits). It is an Econ 101 thing - competition drives down prices/profits.

And here we can see how this looks in practice:

Another aspect that is worth appreciating is the difference in leverage. In the core middle market space, BDCs can also negotiate more favorable credit terms not only on price, but also on the leverage and coverage factor front.

So, by playing in the core middle market area, PFLT can enjoy higher spreads at lower risk.

In my humble opinion, this fact alone should drive premium pricing relative to the sector median; not a situation in which PFLT carries an even slightly lower multiple than P/NAV 0.77x (sector median).

If we look at what the picture looks like on the ground for PFLT, then I guess it would be only fair to conclude that the aforementioned substance really works in practice as well. I will give you three points:

  1. As of Q2 2025, PFLT's portfolio weighted average leverage ratio was 4.3x, and the weighted average interest coverage ratio was 2.5x. These are much stronger leverage and coverage metrics than, say, for widely known BDC blue chips: Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) and Blackstone Secured Lending (NYSE:BXSL).
  2. As of Q2 2025, PFLT had two investments under non-accruals, representing only 0.5% of portfolio market value. This is largely consistent with PFLT's historical average. More importantly, it is well-below sector average of about 2.5%.
  3. 90% of PFLT's portfolio is associated with the absolute safest loan investment category that is possible to obtain in BDC structures - i.e., senior secured first lien. The remaining 10% is linked to equity-like products (there is less than 1% in subordinated debt).

In my view, these aspects should warrant some kind of premium.

In the third point above I mentioned that about 10% of PFLT's portfolio consists of equity products. While there are some solid direct equity co-investment stakes, the bigger driver is the JV participation.

There is an existing JV - PSSL - which also invests in core middle markets. But then PFLT recently announced a formation of a new JV, partnering with Hamilton Lane. The company in Hamilton Lane has already committed $200 million of capital, which in conjunction with $300 million credit facility should secure about $0.5 billion of an additional asset pool in the JV (almost 25% of PFLT's current asset size).

The beauty here is that this funding (as of Q2) was planned to be deployed starting from September/October. Obviously, it takes time to lever up and deploy this kind of capital in the core middle market, but still, the takeaway is that going forward PFLT could benefit greatly from increased dividend flow from this newly formed JV.

Speaking of capital availability and fire power to accommodate growth, we have to factor in the excellent timing of PFLT's equity raise, which happened in Q1, right before the BDC sector-wide sell-off started. PFLT sourced $32 million at an average price of $11.31 per share (about 22% above the current share price).

Because of these dynamics, in a previous earnings call PFLT's management commented on the future dividend streams with the following:

Looking ahead, we are encouraged by the strong outlook for the remainder of the year and anticipate continued NII growth and full dividend coverage.

Considering that as of Q2 2025, the NII per share generation was $0.27 and the dividend per share at $0.31, we can imply that from the incremental capital deployment, PFLT expected to register ~$0.04 per share of growth in the quarterly NII result.

Sure, things have changed since then. The most immediate and perhaps the most material impact will come from lower base rates. And just because of this aspect alone, I would assume that PFLT cuts the current dividend. First, the NII per share is very unlikely to grow in a falling SOFR environment. Second, from the shareholder value creation perspective, it makes more sense to normalize the dividend and use that capital for buying back the shares at such an aggressively discounted price.

The bottom line

PFLT has been unfairly punished by the market.

As described in the main body above, it has much stronger fundamentals than the average BDC, but it is priced even slightly below the average.

On top of that, PFLT has embedded fire power to really expand the portfolio through the combination of timely raised equity (in Q1 before the BDC market tumbled) and a newly established/equitized JV that carries a notable scale in the context of PFLT's own portfolio.

While from the Management commentary we could understand that going into Q3 the expectation was to, in the short-term, grow the NII per share generation by at least $0.04, given how the base rates have evolved, I doubt that this will be possible. So, I am treating this initial expectation on NII per share growth as something that could help PFLT offset the forthcoming pressures, which is yet another luxury that many BDCs simply do not have.

In a nutshell, PFLT looks to me like an attractive high-yield case with high-probability price upside potential that should lead to a relative outperformance.

data.title: Rental Properties Are Overrated - REITs Are The Real Opportunity
data.description:
The Internet is full of influencers praising rental properties as the single best investment.

The general idea is compelling. Use the bank’s money to buy a property and then use the tenant’s rental income to reimburse the bank. If you structure things well, you should be able to be cash flow positive on day 1 and own the property free and clear in 20-30 years from now. By then, the property should also be more valuable due to inflation, and you should also enjoy some tax benefits along the way.

Sounds great, right?

Not so fast.

Yes, real estate can be a great investment, but more often than not, I think that rental properties owned by small individual landlords are bad investments. Here are three reasons why. I will present a better alternative - REITs - at the end.

Reason No. 1: Significant Time Commitment

I often come across various influencers selling real estate investing courses who claim to be able to earn 25%-plus annual returns with their "secret strategies."

But don’t fall for it. Warren Buffett became one of the richest investors on earth thanks to large compounding gains for Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B). If rental property investors were casually earning more than that, we would have many more real estate billionaires.

The reality is that most of these influencers promoting real estate are miscalculating their returns by failing to account for the value of their time and labor.

Real estate is portrayed as a "passive investment," but that’s just not true. You need to first spend countless hours educating yourself and researching your market. You then need to find the right property. Negotiate the deal. Get financing. Renovate the property. Promote it and interview tenants. Then, finally, you need to monitor the tenant and manage the property, and believe me, there's always work: Leaking roofs, insurance claims, neighbor conflicts, vacancies, accounting, taxes, lawyers, contractors, etc.

That’s 100s of hours of work if you want to do it right. This labor has real value and needs to be deducted from your investment returns as you could have spent all this time working extra hours at your company or creating a new business.

Assuming that you valued your time at $40 per hour, and you spent on average two hours per week on your rental (including all the upfront work to find the deal, averaged out over the lifetime of your investment), your labor cost would be $80 per week or $320 per month.

If you deducted that from your returns, you would quickly realize that real estate is not quite as rewarding as you might have initially thought. A big portion of your return is simply the value of your own labor.

Let’s look at an example:

Assume that your rental property earns $1,200 of rental income, which translates to $750 of monthly NOI.

This means that you are effectively earning $430 from your real estate investment and $320 from your own labor.

If you bought the property for $200,000, your unleveraged cash-on-cash would be a mere 3% adjusted for your labor cost. Leverage wouldn’t make it any better as the cap rates would be lower than your interest rate in today’s market.

Long-term growth and appreciation could help get your annual return closer to 10% over time, but the point here is that real estate is not nearly as rewarding as often advertised. Investors are simply failing to account for the value of their own labor.

Studies show that the average leveraged return of private real estate funds is about 8-10% per year, depending on the strategy.

Reason No. 2: Loss of Freedom

Another big issue with rental properties is that they will restrict your time and geographic freedom, which could have a very negative long-term impact on your lifestyle and career.

The impact on your lifestyle is simple to understand. Your plans will often get disrupted by random issues and you won’t be able to freely travel far away for extended periods of time.

But the potential impact on your career is even more important. Your rental properties will tie you down to a specific city, which means you won’t be able to pursue new opportunities as freely as you could have otherwise. If you get a lucrative job offer in NYC tomorrow, you may not be able to take it because your properties and market knowledge are in Las Vegas. Moreover, the properties will also be a major distraction and drain your time and energy, which may then prevent you from advancing in your career and earning that next pay hike. At the very least, it will slow you down as you won’t be as focused, and the negative impact of slower career progress is commonly far more important than whatever your rentals are earning you.

Reason No. 3: Huge Liability Risk

Most investors seem to think that as long as you have an LLC, your liability should be protected, but in reality, it's more complex than that.

The laws are today increasingly tenant-friendly, and there are lots of tenants who know how to take advantage of them to make your life challenging. As an example, they will not hesitate to sue you personally if they deem that you did a poor job at maintaining the property, claiming that you put their life at risk. I know someone who had his tenant sue him because mold had accumulated in the bathroom of his rental due to poor ventilation. The tenant then sued him, claiming that he had gotten sick because of the mold, requesting significant damages. I don’t know what came of it in the end, but I remember him explaining that it was a big headache as it was taking a lot of his time and attention.

Banks will also not hesitate to enforce their rights and sue you personally if you signed the mortgage and fail to make timely payments.

Same for contractors, brokers, property managers, etc.

Lawsuits are a big part of American business culture, and rentals expose you to significant liability risk.

This explains why countless real estate investors file for personal bankruptcy each year. Even successful real estate investors can quickly lose it all due to such liability issues, especially due to the high leverage.

A Better Alternative

I started in private real estate but quickly switched to REITs (VNQ) as I learn more about them.

REITs are publicly listed real estate investment firms that allow you to invest in real estate just like how you would invest in any other industry - through the purchase of stock.

And the reason why I think that they're better investments than private real estate is that studies show that they have historically earned higher returns, and that’s despite being much safer, liquid, and totally passive:

I recently shared an article explaining in detail why REITs are more rewarding, and you can read it by clicking here, but in short, they enjoy huge economies of scale, can use public listings to raise capital and accelerate growth, and often focus on more rewarding specialty property sectors that are out of the reach of individual investors.

Here are three examples of this:

Economies of scale:

Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) is one of the biggest apartment landlords in the sunbelt markets. Its massive scale results in much lower cost at very level, from the management, to leasing, brokerage, maintenance, legal, and everything else.

Real estate is a low-margin business so it's not surprising that these economies of scale can lead to higher returns.

Over the long run, its investors have earned a 36x on their initial investments. Very few private rental investments ever come close to that. The difference is the scale advantage, which leads to higher returns on invested capital:

Public capital access to accelerate growth:

Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) is a net lease REIT that went public in 2018, and it has since then earned its investors near 20% average annual total returns. This is fascinating in that private investors targeting similar properties with comparable leverage would have been happy to earn just half of that.

But EPRT was so much more rewarding because its public listing allowed it to raise additional capital, which it could then reinvest in more properties, earning a positive spread over its cost of capital. This has allowed it to grow its FFO per share by about 8% annually, despite only having 1.5% annual rent escalators in its leases.

Private investors who buy rentals are limited to organic rent growth, and therefore, they cannot compete with REITs like EPRT that are able to boost their growth with such spread investing:

More rewarding specialty property sector:

SBA Communications (SBAC) is a cell tower REIT that has earned its investors a 24x since going public in the late 90s, massively outperforming most real estate investments as well as the S&P500 (SPY).

It was much more rewarding than your typical rental property simply because cell towers enjoyed a strong tailwind in the rapidly growing use of data.

But private investors cannot easily buy cell towers. These assets require significant scale, relationships, and specialized expertise. As a result, these opportunities are left to large investment firms like REITs, which can consistently earn higher returns than traditional rental properties:

Closing Note

Rentals are not as rewarding as most appear to think. They're also much riskier and require a lot of time and effort.

REITs, on the other hand, have historically been more rewarding investments, according to studies, and this makes sense when you consider the above examples.

Best of all, REIT investors enjoyed those returns with lower risk, completely passively, and with geographic freedom, allowing them to focus on their careers to advance faster and optimize their lifestyle.

With that in mind, why would you invest in rental properties? I think that it doesn’t make sense in most cases. Let me know what you think below.

data.title: Vitesse Energy: Dividend Cut Possible, But Undervalued Oil Assets With Potential To Ramp Up Growth
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Vitesse Energy (NYSE:VTS) owns working and mineral interests in oil and gas assets, primarily in the Bakken and Three Forks formations in North Dakota and Montana, funding wells/drilling in return for their share of production revenues. Following a recent acquisition and several macro developments, the company seems to be at a key point right now, with a potential dividend cut coming soon but also many expansion opportunities, so let’s take a deeper look.

Vitesse has interests in over 7,500 productive wells, with an average working interest of 3.6% per working interest well, with over 80% of their assets being undeveloped locations, estimating that they have about 25 years' worth of drilling inventory.

They invest alongside companies like Chord Energy (CHRD) - which I own and recently rated as a Strong Buy in this article - Devon Energy (DVN) which I also own and rated a Strong Buy, Continental, Hess, EOG, and more. They are diversified across more than 30 operators and historically participate in 30% to 55% of the rigs drilling in the basin, being, as they point out, effectively a Bakken operation ETF - one that pays a very generous dividend, I might add, as I mention below.

As we can see, their capital allocation strategy prioritizes their dividend (which I’ll mention below) but also their organic CAPEX for replacements, acquisitions (with a demonstrated track record), as well as repaying their debt, with a target leverage below 1, which is definitely attractive.

Regarding acquisitions, there’s the Lucero acquisition back in March, for which they spent $222 million in an all-stock transaction. I’m generally in favor of this approach over funding it through expensive debt, especially since we’re in an environment where rates are high. They expect about $3 million in synergies per year, primarily related to general & administrative expense reductions, and they said that these assets were already fully integrated during their Q2 Earnings Call:

During the second quarter of '25, we fully integrated the Lucero assets and certain employees into Vitesse, with the accretive impact of apparent in our financial metrics and balance sheet. The asset is performing as expected, and we are realizing better G&A synergies than we underwrote. The operated leg to our strategy provides another lever that we can pull at our discretion.

With limited CAPEX requirements to keep the production flat, they see about $80 million to $110 million in CAPEX this year, which is a relatively conservative amount that should allow them to deliver solid cash flows. That’s compared to ~$94.12 million in CAPEX last year and 13,003 barrels of oil equivalent per day, by the way.

As we can see, they spent nearly $66 million on CAPEX plus acquisition costs, leading to an FCF of about $17.4 million in the first 6 months of 2025, although the adjusted FCF that they report was $21.9 million in Q2 and $9.075 million in Q1. This means that H2 should come with significantly better numbers, as most of their CAPEX target was already spent. Last year, they made around $51.24 million in free cash flow, which is not terrible for their market cap, but it could be an issue for the dividend.

Also, they disclosed that they resolved pending litigation with Hess, after which VTS will receive a one-time cash payment of $24 million, electing to take virtually all of its gas production from Hess-operated wells in-kind commencing July 1, 2025, and has entered into long-term gas gathering, processing, and marketing agreements with Hess affiliates.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see that the current assets are slightly below the current liabilities, although they don’t have that much debt. There’s also $671.87 million in equity on an ~$890 million market cap, but the company’s net asset value is likely higher for the right buyer, depending mostly on the environment.

They also have relatively high insider ownership, with individual insiders owning about 18.5% of Vitesse Energy, which is definitely a plus that aligns the management with the business. Still, it can be risky in the sense that they might be inclined to focus on the stock too much and pay unsustainable dividends or buybacks, for example, just to “milk” it as much as they can, but that doesn’t seem to be the case so far, and the management has been quite disciplined with the capital allocation.

As somewhat of a proof for this management’s ability to secure some value for shareholders, as of Q2, Vitesse has approximately 71% of its remaining 2025 oil production hedged at a weighted average price of $69.83 per barrel and 49% of its remaining 2025 natural gas production hedged at a weighted average floor of $3.73 per MMBtu, on top of other hedges for 2026 and Q1 2027, allowing them to navigate the current environment a bit better.

The company’s next earnings report is scheduled for November 3, and I am looking forward to updates regarding their capital allocation strategy in light of the very volatile market. As I mention below, this could be an optimal time for their management to really shine, especially now that their credit facility is higher thanks to the Lucero acquisition.

Dividends & Buybacks

They maintained a $2.25 dividend annually that was confirmed in Q3, meaning a yield of ~10.27% on the current level, which is definitely not something to ignore. They also have a $60 million buyback program that they plan to use opportunistically.

They paid dividends of $63.6 million, $58.0 million, and $36.0 million in 2024, 2023, and 2022, respectively, but after issuing so much stock and also increasing the dividend this year, that’s costing them roughly $91.4 million per year now, which is far from sustainable. Given the highly volatile environment, I wouldn’t be surprised to see them announce a cut, justifying it with the economic uncertainty they’re going through. Still, they said the following during the Q1 Earnings Report, but things can change:

We're not restricted at all with our credit facility. Our product is our dividend. We feel very confident in our ability to pay the dividend in this pricing environment. So again, it's a decision that we make pretty much on a quarterly basis. But our product is our dividend. We do everything we can to support the dividend and make the decisions to be able to grow the dividend.

They are focused on returning capital to shareholders, but it may not be the best decision, especially since they see a lot of potential deals, as I’ll mention below. Again, the risk of seeing a cut is very real in this case, especially since we might be entering into an environment where their cash flows would struggle, and I personally don’t think a near-term cut would be that bad if it means they can focus on developing the business.

Risks & Opportunities

Ultimately, the main fundamental risks and opportunities of oil-related companies are common. Rate cuts are probably the main near-term opportunity to keep an eye on since they can revitalize the economy, ultimately leading to more activity and demand for oil since people and companies would benefit from cheaper debt and more financial flexibility.

And I’ll leave again the potential catalyst coming from China’s policy changes announced last year, with the first easing since 2010, with potential to see that translate into more demand for oil in China, especially in petrochemicals. They could represent China’s “next stage” of oil demand growth, being used for various plastics/synthetic fibers, and that also means that global rate cuts could be great for China since they’d boost global trade and ultimately lead to more money for Chinese companies.

That also fits their “Made in China” policies for production on top of the quality over quantity improvements. Again, to give you an idea, I’ll include this paragraph written by Ciarán Healy for the IEA:

The speed and scale of the expansion of China’s petrochemical sector dwarfs any historical precedent, roughly doubling the pace of earlier capacity additions in the Middle East and United States. Between 2019 and 2024, China is set to add as much production capacity for ethylene and propylene – the two most important petrochemical building blocks – as presently exists in Europe, Japan and Korea combined.

Another point to keep an eye on is that - on top of OPEC’s production normalizations - Trump’s “Drill, Baby, Drill” policies could actually hurt oil companies by increasing the overall production through more permitting, and we have to keep in mind that he’s after all aiming to lower the price of oil, as he promised before.

As I mentioned before, especially in recent years, oil companies are clearly open to mergers and acquisitions rather than investing significant amounts of money into exploration, which may not be the best thing for VTS. M&As add nothing to the total supply, generally resulting in the same projects being developed but we’re now looking at it under a single company that can integrate these operations and generate synergies. If anything, in this environment, we might be looking at less activity in oil overall, leading to less demand for Vitesse’s properties and limiting their potential to grow. Especially as over 80% of Vitesse’s assets are undeveloped locations, this can be even more accentuated.

Oil prices saw somewhat of a rebound from below $60 following Trump’s new sanctions on Russia, with China initially saying that they also stopped buying Russian oil as a result. According to the news that came shortly after the sanctions, at least 4 state-owned oil giants (PetroChina, Sinopec, CNOOC, and Zhenhua Oil) have suspended purchases of Russian oil shipped by sea, which can overall be good for US oil as well.

However, as good as some catalysts may be, let’s not forget why oil fell below $60 and gold got to nearly $4,400 per ounce in the first place. We had renewed trade war fears with China, tariffs, political uncertainty, OPEC+ removing self-imposed production limits, government shutdowns, enormous and rising amounts of debt, rate cuts, uncertainty around a hard landing, etc. As such, economic activity can be affected, especially in the US, which can then spread globally, meaning less demand for oil.

Again, why this is so important is because the US (followed by China) is the largest producer and consumer of oil on top of a pillar for global trade, meaning that issues there can easily result in issues basically everywhere given enough time.

Still, I would also like to point out that Vitesse could take advantage of this environment, potentially unlocking even better deals at a time when the company’s management is already bullish on acquisitions based on recent calls, with the company’s CFO replying to a question about acquisitions during their Q2 Earnings Call with:

So we just kind of feel pretty comfortable about where we're at. We continue to look at bigger, chunkier deals, and we got a lot of things that we're analyzing and really scrutinizing. But we -- as we've always said, as Bob said earlier in the prepared remarks, we have pretty strenuous requirements when we look at these things. So we're very optimistic given the number of opportunities that are out there but continue to be cautiously optimistic that something will get to the finish line.

And, to make this argument even clearer, they also said the following:

I will say that since we've been in business for 12 years, this is by far the most amount of deal flow we have ever seen in the bigger, chunkier realm. We have also been able to add as the industry consolidates, we're able to add an engineer and an ops guy to our evaluation team, and they're completely busy.

Again, things can get bad for the environment overall and hurt VTS as well, but this is where I’d argue that good management teams can really shine. Still, it’s of course a risk since companies tend to depend on their management even more in bad times, but the right management teams can not only navigate this well, but also help the company emerge even better from the downcycle.

Valuation

Given last year’s $51.24 million in free cash flow, $3 million in synergies from the recent acquisition, the 23% higher production hike (at midpoint), lower oil prices potentially countered by their hedges to some degree, let’s assume $62.5 million in FCF this year, meaning a ~22% increase vs. 2024. I’ll assume a 6% CAGR over the first 5 years to account for their deal-making approach (with the potential to get even more debt to ramp it up), followed by a 4% CAGR until year 10 and a 2% terminal growth rate. Let’s assume a 7.5% WACC (above my estimated 7%; assumptions: ~7.25% cost of equity, ~5.2% cost of debt), as well as $104.04 million in net debt and 40.97 million shares outstanding as of Q2. This way, we get to an enterprise value of $1.4 billion, meaning a $1.29 billion equity value and a stock price of $31.53, which is still above the current level IF they manage to deliver this.

Conclusions

I rate Vitesse Energy a buy now given their financials, valuation, and potential to grow, while noting the industry’s near-term headwinds and the company’s significant risk of cutting the dividend. It would likely get much more attractive after a dividend cut (if they announce it), but the overall investment remains solid regardless. Risk remains, especially in the near term given the industry they play in, but this should definitely be a good stock to consider, especially if the stock price falls even more, potentially unlocking very solid dividends throughout the cycle.

data.title: U.S. CRE Deal Volume Up 17% In Q3 2025
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

MSCI Real Capital Analytics reported that U.S. commercial real estate (CRE) transaction volumes rose +17% in 3Q25, while upwardly revising 2Q25 to +12% (from an initially reported +7%). As a result, total volumes for the first nine months of 2025 were 17% higher than during the same period in 2024.

Transaction volumes often serve as a barometer of market sentiment: accelerating volumes signal growing demand and confidence, while slowing volumes suggest increasing caution. Today’s market still faces notable risks and uncertainties, yet the recent pickup in transaction activity, reminiscent of 2014, is particularly noteworthy. Following the sharp slowdown during the Global Financial Crisis, 2014 marked a turning point: sales activity returned to pre-crisis levels as investor confidence returned. There are hints of a similar inflection point now as property prices are rising again, and there’s renewed investor engagement.

Broad-based strength across property types

Property type 3Q25 YTD
Office 38.4% 24.5%
Industrial 7.5% 15.5%
Retail 23.6% 21.0%
Apartment 12.6% 8.7%
Hotel -14.9% -5.1%
Senior Housing 21.1% 61.5%

Despite the favorable backdrop for year-over-year growth, given easier comparisons in the same period in 2024, 3Q25 likely exceeded expectations for several reasons:

  1. Net operating income (NOI) growth remains above historical averages, driven largely by alternative property types.
  2. Lending markets remain open and liquid, with debt originations up 55% year-to-date as of 2Q25, according to the Mortgage Bankers Association.
  3. The recent tax bill preserved, and in many cases enhanced, the tax advantages of owning U.S. commercial real estate.

YoY comparisons to tighten, momentum holds

We expect transaction volumes to pick up in 4Q25, consistent with the seasonal trend of higher activity in the year’s final quarter. If 4Q25 matches 4Q14 levels at roughly $136 billion, it would mark the highest quarterly volume of the year and push full-year 2025 volumes ~10% higher than 2024.

However, it’s possible that volumes will be flat to slightly lower year-over-year as comparisons will become more challenging into year-end. Transaction volumes totaled $42.0 billion, $36.9 billion, and $62.5 billion in October, November, and December 2024, respectively. This compares with average monthly volumes of $41.7 billion in 3Q25 and $38.6 billion year-to-date in 2025. Notably, December 2024 was higher than every other December since 2001, except during the 2018–2021 period.

Bottom line

The figures are remarkably strong, perhaps even surprisingly so. Few would have expected this performance in the days and months following “Liberation Day.” Earlier concerns that the full effects of inflation had yet to be felt now appear overstated, as both the economy broadly and the CRE market specifically have proven more resilient than anticipated. We think potential headlines that read “volumes slipped YoY in 4Q25” would understate the underlying momentum, as transaction activity accelerated throughout the year. Indeed, the recent pickup in volumes, reminiscent of 2014’s post-crisis recovery, signals a market regaining confidence, with liquidity improving, fundamentals stabilizing, and capital steadily returning to the sector.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: DraftKings: Turning A Competitive Threat Into A Legal Lever (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In early June, I downgraded DraftKings Inc. (NASDAQ:DKNG) to a hold following Illinois’ enactment of a precedent-setting per wager tax on sportsbooks. While the stock rebounded near 52-week highs in August following my last coverage, it is currently down 33% from its August high after a 24% slide over the past month. DraftKings’ latest drop coincided with the beginning of the NFL season, its strongest season, due to the rise of prediction market platforms, namely Kalshi, driving fears over such platforms taking share from incumbent sportsbooks.

Against this backdrop, DraftKings has acted swiftly by acquiring Railbird, a CFTC-licensed designated contract market [DCM], marking its entry into this rapidly growing industry, with the intention of offering event contracts in finance, culture, and entertainment. In the short-term, I expect the acquisition to represent a small, low-margin revenue boost to DraftKings due to the low take rates of prediction market platforms.

In the long-term, however, I expect this acquisition to further accelerate regulators’ efforts to ban DCMs’ sports-related contracts to preserve regulated states’ tax revenue derived from sports wagering. This is the main advantage of the Railbird acquisition, in my opinion, since a ban on DCMs’ sports-related contracts would help DraftKings protect its sportsbook margin, which is substantially higher than DCMs’ margins.

Furthermore, the acquisition could provide DraftKings with leverage in its lobbying efforts in unregulated states, in my opinion, which could be a major boon to its financials in the long-term, especially if sports betting legalization laws are passed in California and Texas. In light of these factors, I’m upgrading DraftKings to a buy with a price target of $53, implying 60% upside from current levels.

Railbird Acquisition

On October 22nd, DraftKings announced its entry into prediction markets with the acquisition of CFTC-licensed DCM Railbird, effectively expanding its TAM into regulated event contracts. The company intends to launch a new app, DraftKings Predictions, in the coming months to allow customers to trade event contracts related to finance, culture, and entertainment. However, the company stated that it may expand into additional categories over time, hinting at the possibility of offering sports-related contracts in the future.

While DraftKings hasn’t shared the acquisition price, I believe it is likely to be an immaterial sum given that the company hasn’t filed an 8-K announcing the acquisition, a required step for public companies when announcing material public information.

DraftKings’ acquisition of Railbird follows CME Group’s (CME) partnership with Flutter’s (FLUT) FanDuel on prediction and event contracts that will initially start with financial contracts only. This trend of leading online sportsbooks expanding into prediction markets highlights the growing popularity and threat of DCMs’ sports event contracts, mainly offered by Kalshi and Polymarket.

With that in mind, sports events have driven substantial trading volume on prediction markets’ platforms recently with the start of the NFL season. Data from Dune Analytics states that notional trading volume exceeded $2 billion on Polymarket and Kalshi for the week ended October 19.

This substantial volume was mainly driven by the MLB playoffs and the start of the NHL season, as well as the ongoing NFL and college football seasons. As a result of this strong demand, Kalshi is now expected to reach an annualized trading volume of $50 billion later this year, enough for a 60% share of the global market, surpassing Polymarket in the process.

The main contributor to prediction markets platforms’ robust growth in sports events contracts is their CFTC license which allows them to offer their services across all 50 states, including markets with unregulated sports betting. Kalshi and Polymarket have been operating on the premise that since they are regulated by the CFTC, they don’t require individual state licenses from the states in which they operate, unlike sportsbooks that require licenses to operate in states, in addition to paying state taxes.

Railbird: A Small Price To Pay For Legal Clarity?

Therefore, DraftKings’ latest acquisition of Railbird is a major positive, in my opinion, since it could provide it with regulatory leverage over DCMs in regulated and unregulated states. In my opinion, it is unlikely that DraftKings offers sports-related contracts following its Railbird acquisition, even in unregulated markets, for 2 main reasons.

The first reason is to not attract scrutiny from state regulators, especially in unregulated markets, to maintain regulatory support in regulated states and its ability to lobby unregulated markets in the future. The second reason is the financial hit DraftKings’ core business could take if it offers sports-related event contracts.

Prediction market platforms are an extremely low margin business despite the significant trading volume on their platforms since they only generate revenue through fees they charge per trade. These platforms average a take rate of around 1% of trading volume, whereas DraftKings last reported an 8.7% take rate on its handle in Q2. This means that if the company sacrifices a portion of its sportsbook handle to its anticipated DraftKings Predictions platform, it risks losing a significant portion of its revenue.

As such, it is highly likely that DraftKings’ acquisition of Railbird is to accelerate the ongoing regulatory scrutiny over DCMs’ sports-related contracts. Several states, including Nevada, Arizona, Illinois, Maryland, New Jersey, Montana, and Ohio, have all issued cease-and-desists to Kalshi. The most notable outcomes were in Maryland, where the state secured a win after a US district judge denied Kalshi’s motion for a preliminary injunction, allowing the state’s cease-and-desist order to stand.

Additionally, the Massachusetts Attorney General filed a civil lawsuit against Kalshi, alleging that its platform engages in illegal sports betting operations without a license, seeking an injunction to stop sports contracts in the state. Meanwhile, tribal governments in California and Wisconsin have also filed lawsuits against prediction market platforms, citing concerns over their impact on tribes’ exclusive gaming rights, which represents a violation of tribal compacts.

Concurrently, a group of US senators sent a letter to the CFTC, urging the agency to clarify that sports prediction markets are functionally sports gambling and, thus, aren’t preempted by federal law and are subject to state and tribal regulations. In this letter, the senators argued that the CFTC is prohibited from allowing event contracts that involve gaming, are unlawful under federal or state law, or are contrary to the public interest under the Commodity Exchange Act.

At the same time, a group of Attorney Generals and regulators representing more than 30 states have signed on to amicus briefs in the ongoing litigation against Kalshi. These briefs urge the federal appellate courts to side with the states, arguing that the CFTC’s jurisdiction over derivatives doesn’t extend to regulating or legalizing activities that states classify as gambling.

Given state regulators’ efforts to stop sports-related event contracts, I believe the main purpose of DraftKings’ acquisition of Railbird and FanDuel’s partnership with CME is to accelerate these efforts to maintain their existing duopoly in online sports betting. With the 2 largest online sportsbooks expanding into prediction markets, regulators are likely to push further for legislation that effectively bans or regulates DCMs’ sports-related contracts, in order to preserve the tax revenue generated from sports betting, which has been a major boon to regulated states’ finances, as I mentioned in my last coverage.

Race To Legalization

Another potential advantage to DraftKings’ acquisition of Railbird and FanDuel’s partnership with CME is that both actions could pave the way for online sports betting legalization in states yet to legalize the activity, namely California and Texas. Both California and Texas are expected to be the largest sports betting markets in the US, with a combined population of more than 70.7 million, yet they remain untapped due to both states having unregulated sports betting.

In California, sports betting legalization faces a major barrier in the immense power of Native American tribes, who hold exclusive rights to operate casino gaming and view online sports betting as an existential threat to their profitable casino exclusivity. Meanwhile in Texas, the barrier to online sports betting is conservative political opposition in the state legislature as well as the need for a constitutional amendment to pass before legalizing sports betting.

However, both states’ approaches to online sports betting legalization could take a different turn after the rapid rise of prediction market sports-related contracts from Kalshi and Polymarket. This is likely to be the case due to Kalshi marketing its sports contracts with ads stating that sports betting is legal in all 50 states, including California and Texas.

With DraftKings and FanDuel announcing their own prediction markets platforms, both companies now have a significant lobbying tool, in my opinion, in states that are yet to legalize online sports betting. This can be done by forcing regulatory decisions that either legalize and tax sports betting under states’ control or allow federally-licensed DCMs to grow their market shares in these markets, effectively losing the tax revenue derived from sports betting.

With that in mind, if California and Texas introduce their versions of sports betting legalization laws, such laws are very likely to pass given that 60% of Californians and Texans already support sports betting legalization.

Valuation

Given the regulatory leverage DraftKings now holds against DCMs and unregulated sports betting states following its Railbird acquisition, the stock appears to be mispriced, in my opinion. At the stock’s current share price of $33, DraftKings has a market cap of $16.4 billion, which translates to an EV of nearly $17 billion after taking its $574.3 million net debt position into consideration.

As such, the stock is trading at a forward EV/sales multiple of 2.69 at the midpoint of DraftKings’ FY 2025 revenue guidance of $6.3 billion. With that in mind, DraftKings’ median 3-year EV/sales multiple is 4.24, suggesting that fears of competition from Kalshi and Polymarket may be overstated, in my opinion, especially post the Railbird acquisition. I believe this could lead to a rerating of DraftKings’ shares in the short-term, which can be driven by strong results when the company reports Q3 earnings on November 7th.

By applying DraftKings’ 3-year median EV/sales multiple to the midpoint of its FY 2025 revenue guidance, my price target for the stock is $52.65, which represents a potential 60% upside.

Risks

The most significant risk to my bullish thesis on DraftKings is that the ongoing litigation against DCMs fails to secure a clear ruling on the legal status of sports-related contracts. If the CFTC’s jurisdiction is upheld, low-margin DCMs will continue to compete with DraftKings’ higher-margin sportsbook, which could force the company to indulge in margin-dilutive sports-related contracts in its upcoming DraftKings Predictions app.

Another risk worth considering is sports leagues potentially placing strict limits on player prop bets or regulators implementing restrictions on prop bets following the latest high-profile NBA arrests. Such a scenario could have a devastating impact on DraftKings’ handle, given the popularity of these bets, as 73% of bettors already participate in prop bets. At the same time, sportsbooks may have to invest in technologies to flag suspicious prop betting patterns to reduce the occurrence of similar activities in the future, which would impact DraftKings’ bottom line.

Conclusion

In summary, DraftKings’ acquisition of Railbird not only expands its TAM, but it also provides it with significant regulatory leverage to accelerate policymakers’ efforts to ban or regulate sports-related event contracts and to push unregulated markets to pass laws legalizing sports betting. In my opinion, the acquisition and its potential regulatory advantages resolve the overblown competitive fears from DCMs that have driven the stock’s 32% fall over the past few months.

As such, I expect shares of DraftKings to witness a rerating in the short term as the market realizes the benefits of the Railbird acquisition, which could be driven by strong Q3 earnings on November 7th. Based on these factors, I’m upgrading DraftKings to a buy with a price target of $53, representing 60% upside from its current valuation.
